candidate_problems.text: What exact formal conditions are load-bearing for the claimed analogue to incompleteness in agent self-knowledge? The conjecture says 'self-reference plus sufficient expressive power blocks closure,' but it remains open which ingredients are actually necessary and sufficient: arithmetic strength, a proof system, semantic truth predicates, reflective oracles, predictive self-models, control-theoretic feedback, or only some weaker diagonalizable structure. Making this precise would test whether the analogy is genuinely about Gödel-style incompleteness or about a broader family of limits on reflective control.

How should 'truths about the agent's own operation' be defined when the agent is embedded, resource-bounded, and acting under uncertainty? The conjecture moves from metaphysical self-knowledge to internal model-based control, but leaves unresolved whether the relevant notion is proof-theoretic derivability, semantic accuracy, calibration of self-prediction, policy reliability, or successful self-guidance. This matters because different notions may yield different impossibility results, and some may evade classical incompleteness while still exhibiting practical blind spots.

Can architectures be designed that do not achieve complete self-certification but still obtain robust, useful forms of reflective self-governance, and what are the tradeoffs? The conjecture identifies a structural limit, but the open systems question is what stable substitutes are possible: hierarchical oversight, probabilistic self-trust, external certification loops, modular self-models, or corrigible uncertainty about one's own reasoning. Exploring this would turn the conjecture from a negative thesis into a design theory for reflective agents.
